item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article was non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it was taken and copyrighted by photographer Chris Kotsiopoulos (as shown here), making it non-compliant. As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Who would win?
A phenomenal lightning storm with the strength of several hurricanes one unlimited-potato bag boi

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think they said "In a site with an infinite sack of potatoes, this article was the one that broke my suspension of disbelief" I believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um, I don’t follow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m just was that in a site with a sack of infinite potatoes this is the article that shot my suspension of disbelief to hell. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup. Broke my suspension as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like 99% of this. The 1% is the absurd implausibility of having 36 teams of actors with these precise characteristics, and unfortunately that's enough to ruin an otherwise great skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is by far the stupidest skip I've read in a very long time.
And yet, you had me at "squirrel wrangler." I was really not expecting something this outrageously silly. Take your +1 and go.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I suggest changing "via anomalous means" to read "through other means." Apart from that, the link works for me, and I approve. Please proceed.;

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So here we have THE bad weather skip. One that is both incredibly dangerous, and frankly ridiculous to contain. That in mind, I'd like to think the Foundation basically reached a point where they were throwing everything at the wall to see what stuck with respect to containment, before they found a magic bullet with Protocol 1746-Aleph. This would include attempts to disappaite SCP-1746 with an instance of SCP-853. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

The development of Protocol 1746-Aleph originated after an instance of SCP-1746 formed in Texas on May ██, 1966 but suddenly dissipated. Foundation climatologists who had been studying SCP-1746 before that time had hypothesized that it may be possible to disrupt an instance of SCP-1746 by generating appropriate atmospheric and electromagnetic stimulus; the dissipation of that instance appeared to confirm the hypothesis and pose the question of how the stimuli had, in this case, been serendipitously produced. Fortunately, a television news crew that had been filming for stock footage purposes in the town of ████ (within the projected path of the SCP-1746 instance) had recorded a sequence of events which Foundation climatologists, statisticians and mathematicians were able to verify as producing the required stimulus. The Foundation was subsequently able to develop Protocol 1746-Aleph by studying the film and reproducing the necessary series of events to generate the stimuli. Protocol 1746-Aleph, therefore, represents a deliberate effort to produce the disruptive stimulus by artificially and accurately re-creating the precise sequence of events that generated the stimulus in 1966.

To this:

The development of Protocol 1746-Aleph originated after an instance of SCP-1746 formed in Texas on May ██, 1966 but suddenly dissipated. Foundation climatologists who had been studying SCP-1746 before that time had hypothesized that it may be possible to disrupt an instance of SCP-1746 by generating appropriate atmospheric and electromagnetic stimulus; the dissipation of that instance appeared to confirm the hypothesis and pose the question of how the stimuli had, in this case, been serendipitously produced. Fortunately, a television news crew that had been filming for stock footage purposes in the town of ████ (within the projected path of the SCP-1746 instance) had recorded a sequence of events which Foundation climatologists, statisticians and mathematicians were able to verify as producing the required stimulus. The Foundation was subsequently able to develop Protocol 1746-Aleph by studying the film and reproducing the necessary series of events to generate the stimuli. Protocol 1746-Aleph, therefore, represents a deliberate effort to produce the disruptive stimulus by artificially and accurately re-creating the precise sequence of events that generated the stimulus in 19661.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Prior attempts to disrupt SCP-1746 instances through other means, including controlled release of SCP-853, had unilaterally resulted in failure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I concur - I do love a well written Noodle Incident :D +1 here also

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly don't think the Foundation has enough resources to scrounge up all that many men under the age of 34 who have adult daughters, let alone men under the age of 34 who don't have dwarfism who have adult daughters who do have dwarfism.

Since, you know, he woulda have to have been at most 14 when he had the kid. And even if you find a few, you're probably only going to be able to keep them on the staff for a year or two max before swapping them out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god someone make a keter lettuce skip

We also have Euclid zucchini, which may evolve its way up to Keter soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god someone make a keter lettuce skip

We have Keter cucumbers. I forget the number.

I was thinking of the Euclid zucchini. I thought they were Keter Cucumbers. *redface*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
During my reading, I REALLY hoped that Protocol 1746-Aleph would not be at the end of the document as [DATA EXPUNGED], which would guarantee a big fat downvote from me.

However, it wasn't and you provided a "logical" ending and explanation of what is going on, even leaving out the description of the procedure in an elegant way. You deserve a +1 for not taking the easy way out from ridiculously random containment procedures and still coming full circle with the SCP, not leaving me disappointed and wanting to know more about 1746-Aleph, because it is all explained in the procedures. The people just are there, doing stuff and that's all I needed to know. Bravissimo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts exactly. After reading the containment procedures, I thought - hey, those are basically more peaceful 231 containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm imagining someone yelling that and rolling on the floor laughing next to my laptop
Oh god someone make a keter lettuce skip

Anyway
At first I thought it would be your average over the top killer storm, maybe intelligent.
Then I hit this:

Seven (7) trained actors

At that point I wondered if this was like the Egotistical Meteorite (or whatever that -J was that needed the "SCIENCE!!!" acting seminar).
Then I read the rest
Loved it
Upvoted with no regrets!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would be brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The most interesting thing to me is the way some details are ultra-specific but others are pointedly not important, F1's weight for example.
This implies that the Foundation didn't just recreate the scene and slap a "Do Not Touch" label on it. There would have to be some extensive trial and error for them to figure out that F1 could be anorexic or obese and no worries, but Oh My God! Tell me that isn't iceberg lettuce! RUN FOR YOUR LIVES!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's less a question of having the resources to find them as it is a matter of does that incredibly specific family combination even exist in the first place. Also consider that they need 36 of these pairs.

Then there is the time pressure. A Zeus' Tantrum may form at any moment. To keep it from turning all of Oklahoma into an ozone-reeking scorch mark, a small female must drop her ice cream. It would be great if we could have only consenting adults handle this; but it's clearly the lesser evil to risk the life of one child when the alternative is the death of hundreds or thousands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure the Foundation has enough resources to find someone like that, rather than, y'know, resorting to using children.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're right about reclassification. The storm itself is dangerous enough to be reclassified as Keter, despite the odd containment procedures.

The change cup carried by Actor M3 (the hobo) must contain only coins satisfying pre-Coinage Act of 1965 metallurgical standards.

I see what you did there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the containment is the slapstick-comedy cousin to 110-Montauk?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly. And right, i missed the maximum. Not only that, though. They need to find a qualifying adult with dwarfism, whose father is less than 34 years of age, meets the minimum weight requirement (almost certainly requiring that the father NOT have dwarfism), AND meets specific genetic conditions.

Oh, and they both have to be able to act.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. I just… wow. I REALLY want to know what that procedure is. Of course, I understand that I'll probably think it's far more bizarre than anything that can be written down as long as I DON'T know.

Take your upvote, hang it on the wall. Let it be a medal of honor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maximum mass. If they're not going to use a child, they need to use a qualifying adult woman with dwarfism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem is that not just any adult could do it. The proceedures state that F3 must be a descendant of m4 and just over 54 lbs. Finding an adult (or rather, a pair, including m4) that meets that requirement is no small feat, though it would be easier to control the mass on a fully grown individual. Point is, the foundation probably made do with what they had, and after the loss decided the risk was too great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fucking love this. I love that the better-safe-than-sorry nature of the Foundation just has them not even bother trying to find out what, precisely, in the sequence of events is actually causing the dissipation. I politely disagree with Azzleflux above; I think that, in this case, the specific procedures weren't that ridiculous (just re-enacting a sequence of normal events over and over). +1 to infinity and beyond!

E: dear self: lern 2 english lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not digging it. I'm probably biased because the main hook is specific, ridiculous procedures, but it really just felt like a really weak impact, and the fact that you apparently had the Foundation using a child for the procedures just irks me on so many levels, mostly because you explicitly state that an adult could do the same job (unless you were talking about the squirrel?).

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any other lettuce is strictly prohibited.

Oooohh yess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, so crazy. It feels like it'd strain plausibility less if it had something implying that it took more than one try to get the protocol right given how precise it seems to be, but that doesn't bug me nearly enough to not upvote.

Love it, A++++++, would suspend disbelief again. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Best headcanon for best containment procedures/failure thereof.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what the shit
i love you
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely motherfuck yes. +1 to the grave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually started thinking that myself after going through SCPs.

Without magic the world the Foundation resides in seems doomed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is ludicrous in the best possible ways. I've always adored the idea that, despite all of their resources, the Foundation is constantly overlooking something incredibly fundamental about the things they contain, which is why they grasp so desperately to whatever works regardless of how insane it is. Sometimes that insanity looks like 110-Montauk, and sometimes it looks like this.

The best part, per my headcanon, is that if the Foundation would just bite the bullet and hire a few competent Serpent's Hand types as consultants to look this over, most of it would prove monstrously unnecessary. I picture a conversation something along the lines of, "You know you could just have the little girl throw a drop of squirrel milk over her shoulder and do away with the rest of this right?"

In any case, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pickiest fucking storms ever. Also, that cast list sounds like the most scientifically-formatted "walks into a bar" joke setup ever.

I love how this is like 80% containment, even in the description. And as wacky as they are, the procedures leave enough out to still be intriguing. Goes to show not everything has to be Montauk to work. I also love that this makes me wonder just how much of that procedure is actually necessary. I mean, did they just start doing the exact same thing or have they tested it at all? Can they even test it, given that failure is generally going to be catastrophic?

My one question: why is "DOCUMENT" in all caps?

Oh, and I feel like this could qualify as Keter, given how much can go wrong in the containment. Begin discussion!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Squirrel 2 failed to carry the frozen dairy confection to its designated mark before consuming it.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastically bizarre, and I love it. It's one gigantic Noodle Incident, and despite the fact that it's therefore basically just playing a very old trope to the hilt, it's nevertheless very well-executed. Also, I spent the entire article torn between "LOL" and "WTF?"; that strong an emotional response is often grounds for an upvote in and of itself.

Well done, sir. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Coulda sworn it was a repost, turns out I was right. I find it much more plausible and straightforward this time around, and the noodle incident elements jive with me. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me at "squirrel wrangler." +1

I really like the butterfly effect element to this. It's a clever twist on containment. And agreement with drewbear, best containment failure ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That may be the most hilariously tragic reason for a containment failure ever.

I do like this one better than the original version.
